claims.claim: CSS-native masonry layout can be achieved with a single property — `grid-template-rows: masonry` — requiring no JavaScript.
claims.quote: No JavaScript required for basic masonry layouts when browser support exists

claims.claim: As of the article's 2021 writing, CSS grid masonry was only available in Firefox Nightly behind a feature flag (`layout.css.grid-template-masonry-value.enabled`), not in stable browsers.
claims.quote: Firefox Nightly only, requiring the `layout.css.grid-template-masonry-value.enabled` flag enabled via `about:config`

claims.claim: A May 2023 update to the article notes the feature is still experimental, with separate Chrome and WebKit proposals still in progress — meaning production use remained blocked years after initial proposal.
claims.quote: This article, dated 2021, presents information from its experimental phase.

claims.claim: Progressive enhancement is possible via `@supports (grid-template-rows: masonry)` to serve the masonry layout only to browsers that support it.
claims.quote: Feature detection available: Using `@supports (grid-template-rows: masonry)` or `CSS.supports("grid-template-rows", "masonry")`

claims.claim: Combining column-spanning items with masonry may produce layout gaps — the author had to explicitly work around gap behavior when spanning multiple columns.
claims.quote: for some reason I wanted the grid to nicely close the items together — suggesting potential layout gaps when combining spans with masonry
